title: Coffee and walnut cake
subtitle: The classic British café cake, layered with coffee buttercream
excerpt: Two 20 cm coffee sponges, the batter flavoured with dissolved instant coffee, sandwiched and iced with a coffee-butter icing. Walnuts go into the sponge and the decoration. The buttercream needs to be firm enough to hold its shape; this means using icing sugar and butter in the right ratio.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the coffee cake chapter documents coffee-flavoured sponges and the butter icing tradition. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the layered sponge cakes with flavoured fillings are documented throughout. The coffee and walnut combination became a British tearoom standard from the mid-twentieth century; the technique here : the Victoria sponge ratio of equal weights flour, butter, sugar, and eggs, flavoured with dissolved coffee draws directly from the public-domain British creamed-sponge tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the un-iced sponges wrapped tightly. Defrost overnight at room temperature and ice on the day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sponges bake up to 2 days ahead; wrap well and store at room temperature. The icing keeps refrigerated for 3 days; beat again briefly before using.

recipeTools.notes: Two 20 cm sandwich tins, greased and lined.

glossaryTerms.definition: Beating butter and sugar together until the mixture is pale, fluffy, and has roughly doubled in volume. The beating incorporates air into the butter, which expands in the oven and lifts the sponge. Under-creamed butter produces a dense, close-textured cake. Full creaming takes 4–6 minutes at medium-high speed with a stand mixer.

content.text: Coffee and walnut cake is a Victoria sponge with coffee in the batter and buttercream. The ratio is the same of equal weights of butter, sugar, flour, and eggs and the method is the creaming method that the Victoria sponge uses. What changes is the coffee, which goes in dissolved in a tablespoon of hot water so it distributes evenly through the batter, and the walnuts, which go into the batter and on top.

is the work. Butter and sugar beaten until properly pale and fluffy (not just mixed) makes the difference between a light, airy sponge and a dense one. In a stand mixer this takes 5 minutes at medium-high; by hand it takes longer. The sponge that follows is straightforward: fold in the flour with a light hand, add the eggs gradually so the batter doesn't curdle, bake until a skewer comes out clean.

items.prepNote: dissolved in 1 tablespoon of hot water
dissolved in 2 teaspoons of hot water

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Grease two 20 cm sandwich tins and line the bases. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} softened butter and {{caster-sugar}} caster sugar together until pale, very fluffy, and roughly doubled in volume — about 5 minutes at medium-high speed with a stand or hand mixer. The mixture should look almost white and leave a clean trail when the beaters are lifted.

Add the {{eggs}} eggs one at a time, beating well after each addition. If the mixture looks as though it is about to curdle (the batter separates and looks lumpy), add a tablespoon of flour before adding the next egg. This stabilises the emulsion. Fold in the {{self-raising-flour}} sifted flour in two stages using a large metal spoon or silicone spatula, using a figure-of-eight folding motion to preserve the air beaten in during creaming. Add the dissolved {{coffee-instant}} coffee and the {{walnuts}} chopped walnuts with the last of the flour.

Divide the batter evenly between the prepared tins and smooth the tops. Bake for 25–28 minutes, until the sponges are golden-brown, spring back when pressed lightly in the centre, and have pulled away slightly from the tin sides. A skewer through the centre should come out clean. Cool in the tins for 10 minutes, then transfer to a rack to cool completely.

Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} softened butter for 2 minutes until pale. Sift in the {{icing-sugar}} icing sugar in two stages, beating on low then increasing the speed. Add the dissolved coffee and beat until smooth and spreadable. If the buttercream is very soft, refrigerate for 10 minutes. Spread half the buttercream on the underside of one sponge, sandwich with the other sponge, then use the remaining buttercream to cover the top and sides or just the top according to preference. Arrange the {{walnuts}} walnut halves on top.
